content.text:
@[[CLAUDE]] 

Grounded explanation: **Fourier & sampling** (FT 2D, Nyquist, FFT, downsampling)

# Fourier & sampling — a grounded study sweep These four ideas — **2D FT**, **Nyquist**, **FFT**, **downsampling** — are really one chain of reasoning. The 2D FT gives you a frequency view of the image; Nyquist tells you what frequencies you can even measure on a discrete grid; the FFT is the algorithm that makes the FT computable in practice; and downsampling is the operation that makes Nyquist *bite* — if you do it carelessly, you get aliasing. ## 1. The 2D Fourier transform The 2D FT decomposes an image $g(x,y)$ into a sum of complex 2D plane waves $e^{2\pi i(x f_x + y f_y)}$. Each frequency $(f_x, f_y)$ is a single complex number — its **magnitude** says how strongly that wave is present, its **phase** says where it's positioned. The forward and inverse pair (slide 2): $$\hat{g}(f_x, f_y) = \int g(x,y)\, e^{-2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}\, dx\, dy$$ $$g(x,y) = \int \hat{g}(f_x, f_y)\, e^{2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}\, df_x\, df_y$$ Two duality facts to internalise: - **One pixel in Fourier space contains information about every pixel in real space**, and vice versa. There is no spatial locality in $\hat{g}$. - We almost always visualise the **magnitude** $|\hat{g}|$, not phase, and we plot $\log(|\hat{g}|+1)$ because natural-image spectra decay roughly as $1/\sqrt{f_x^2+f_y^2}$ — without the log the centre saturates and the rest is black. **Radial structure.** Frequencies with the same magnitude $|f|=\sqrt{f_x^2+f_y^2}$ form **concentric rings** around DC. Centre = low frequencies (slow variations, overall shape), outer rings = high frequencies (edges, fine detail, noise). This is what lets you describe a "low-pass mask" as a disc and a "band-pass" as an annulus. **fftshift convention.** A raw 1D FFT for $N=7$ orders frequencies as $[0, \tfrac{1}{7}, \tfrac{2}{7}, \tfrac{3}{7}, -\tfrac{3}{7}, -\tfrac{2}{7}, -\tfrac{1}{7}]$. After `fftshift` they're in natural order $[-\tfrac{3}{7},\dots,\tfrac{3}{7}]$. In 2D this puts DC at the centre — required for any spectrum image to look right and for circular masks to be centred. ## 2. Sampling and the Nyquist bound This is the part that motivates everything else. Sampling a continuous signal at spacing $x_0$ is mathematically the same as **multiplying** it by an impulse train of period $x_0$. By the convolution theorem, multiplication in the spatial domain is **convolution in the frequency domain** — and the FT of an impulse train of period $x_0$ is itself an impulse train, of period $1/x_0$. So sampling **replicates the spectrum** of the original signal at intervals of $1/x_0$ along the frequency axis. If the original spectrum had support $|u| \leq u_\text{max}$, the copies don't overlap iff $$\frac{1}{x_0} \geq 2 u_\text{max} \qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad u_\text{max} \leq \frac{1}{2 x_0}$$ That is the **Nyquist bound** (slide 48). $1/x_0$ is your sampling rate; $2 u_\text{max}$ is the **Nyquist rate**. If you sample more slowly than Nyquist, the spectral copies overlap and high frequencies fold back into the low-frequency region — **aliasing**. The classic example is the moiré on the building façade in slide 45: the camera grid is too coarse for the brick pattern, and the brick frequency aliases into a slow, swirling pattern that wasn't there. The practical rule: if the smallest feature you care about has size $x$, sample at spacing $\leq x/2$. *Smallest feature 10 nm → measure every 5 nm or finer.* ```mermaid flowchart LR cont["continuous F(u),
support |u| ≤ u_max"] -->|"× impulse train
period x₀"| sampled["F_s(u): copies
spaced 1/x₀"] sampled --> q{"1/x₀ ≥ 2 u_max?"} q -->|"yes"| ok["copies don't overlap
→ recoverable"] q -->|"no"| alias["overlap → aliasing
(moiré)"] ``` ## 3. The FFT — what makes this practical The discrete 2D FT, computed naively from the integral pair above, costs $O(N^2)$ per pixel for an $N\times N$ image, i.e. $O(N^4)$ overall. The **Fast Fourier Transform** is an algorithm (radix-2 Cooley–Tukey is the canonical one) that exploits redundancy in the DFT matrix to compute the same result in $O(N \log N)$ per axis — total $O(N^2 \log N)$ for a 2D image. That's the difference between minutes and milliseconds. The lecture leans on this complexity in the **convolution pipeline** (slide 23): instead of doing a direct spatial convolution at $O(n \cdot k)$ for signal length $n$ and kernel length $k$, you go FFT → pointwise multiply → IFFT, paying $O(n \log n)$ each way. This is faster than direct convolution once $k$ is large, which is why frequency-domain filtering is the default for big kernels (e.g. wide Gaussian blurs, large band-pass filters). ```mermaid flowchart LR F["input f, length n"] -->|"FFT O(n log n)"| Fhat["f̂"] G["kernel g, length k≤n"] -->|"FFT O(k log k)"| Ghat["ĝ"] Fhat --> M["pointwise multiply O(n)"] Ghat --> M M --> P["f̂ · ĝ"] P -->|"IFFT O(n log n)"| H["h = f * g"] ``` *(Beyond the lecture, but worth knowing: the FFT is what makes anything in this lecture — convolution theorem, Wiener deconvolution, Fourier resizing, sub-pixel shift — actually usable on real images. The lecture treats it as a black box and just credits the $O(n \log n)$ cost.)* ## 4. Downsampling — where Nyquist becomes operational You can downsample an $L \times L$ image to $L' \times L'$ in **frequency space** by cropping the spectrum (slide 29): $$\hat{I}_\text{new}(u,v) = \begin{cases} \hat{I}(u,v) & \text{if } |u|, |v| \leq L'/2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ then inverse-transforming. Because you've **deleted** the high frequencies before reconstructing on the smaller grid, anti-aliasing is **built in**: there's nothing left to fold back. This is why spectrum-crop downsampling is "automatically anti-aliased." The complementary operation is **upsampling by zero-padding** the spectrum: surround $\hat{I}$ with zeros to reach $L'\times L'$, then IFFT. This gives you an exact band-limited (sinc-interpolated) reconstruction at the higher resolution — theoretically the ideal interpolant for a band-limited signal. The contrast that makes Nyquist matter: if you instead downsample naively in the spatial domain (just keep every $k$-th pixel), you have effectively **resampled** at a coarser grid without first removing the high frequencies. The new sampling rate is below the original $2u_\text{max}$, the spectral copies overlap, and you get aliasing. The standard fix in the spatial domain is **blur first, then decimate** — the blur is a low-pass that enforces a band-limit compatible with the new grid. That's the same idea as spectrum-crop, just done in real space. ```mermaid flowchart LR img["image I(x,y),
L×L"] -->|"FFT2"| spec["Î(u,v)"] spec -->|"keep |u|,|v|≤L'/2,
zero outside"| cropped["Î_new"] cropped -->|"IFFT2"| out["downsampled I,
L'×L'"] ``` ## How they chain together ```mermaid flowchart TD cont["Continuous image"] -->|"sample at spacing x₀"| disc["Discrete image"] disc -->|"FFT"| spec["Spectrum Î(u,v)"] spec --> use["Filtering, convolution,
shift, deconvolution"] nyq["Nyquist: 1/x₀ ≥ 2u_max"] -.->|"violated → aliasing"| disc spec -->|"crop"| down["Downsampled
(anti-aliased)"] spec -->|"zero-pad"| up["Upsampled
(sinc-interpolated)"] ``` --- **The source's exact framing** *Lecture 10 (`10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/lecture-10-fourier-transform-2.md`) defines the 2D FT pair on slide 2 and emphasises radial frequency $|f|=\sqrt{f_x^2+f_y^2}$ on slide 7, with natural-image magnitude decaying as $1/\sqrt{f_x^2+f_y^2}$. Sampling theory (slides 46–48) is framed as: sampling = multiplication by an impulse train, which by the convolution theorem becomes spectrum replication at intervals of $1/x_0$; Nyquist's bound is stated as $u_\text{max} \leq \tfrac{1}{2 x_0}$. Spectrum-crop downsampling is given on slide 29 as $\hat{I}_\text{new}(u,v)=\hat{I}(u,v)$ for $|u|,|v|\leq L'/2$ and $0$ otherwise. The FFT itself is invoked only as the $O(n\log n)$ kernel inside the convolution pipeline diagram (slide 23) — the lecture doesn't derive Cooley–Tukey.* **Remember this** Sampling at spacing $x_0$ replicates the spectrum every $1/x_0$; copies don't overlap iff $1/x_0 \geq 2u_\text{max}$ (Nyquist) — and spectrum-crop downsampling is automatically anti-aliased because it deletes the high frequencies *before* you resample. **Beyond the lecture** I added the explicit $O(N^2\log N)$ cost for the 2D FFT and the framing of "blur-then-decimate" as the spatial-domain analogue of spectrum-crop — the lecture covers spectrum-crop and lists FFT complexity for 1D convolution but doesn't draw that bridge directly.
